requirements: The core contributions of HEXGEN-FLOW have been replicated, including the agentic Text-to-SQL workflow, the two-level scheduling architecture, and reproduction of reported latency and throughput improvements.

sub_tasks.requirements: Models, datasets, and hardware required for evaluation are available.
The LLAMA3.1-70B model has been obtained and configured for serving with vLLM using tensor parallelism degree of eight.
The QWEN3-30B model has been obtained for the alternative-workflow evaluation on MAC-SQL.
The BIRD-bench development set has been downloaded and the financial and formula1 databases are accessible for trace construction.
The Spider 1.0 dataset has been obtained for sampling Trace 4 with 9 new-domain databases (dog kennels, cars, etc.).
Hetero-1 deployment is set up: two A100 GPUs and two A6000 GPUs, hosting four model instances total with four GPUs allocated per instance.
Hetero-2 deployment is set up: A100 hosting two model instances, plus one L40 instance and one A6000 instance, each with four GPUs per instance.
Homo deployment is set up: an A100-only cluster hosting four model instances, each using four GPUs.
vLLM is installed and configured as the underlying LLM inference engine with continuous batching enabled on each model instance.
The agentic Text-to-SQL workflows used in the evaluation have been implemented.
The CHESS agentic Text-to-SQL pipeline has been implemented as the primary workflow used in main experiments.
Schema linking stage is implemented: given table metadata and column descriptions, the LLM identifies and aligns entities in the natural-language query to relevant tables and columns.
SQL candidate generation stage is implemented: a fixed number of candidate SQL statements are generated via multiple concurrent LLM inference requests using different prompts/examples.
Self-correction stage is implemented: candidate SQLs are executed against the database and iteratively refined via subsequent LLM calls up to a maximum of 10 iterations.
Evaluation stage is implemented: the LLM synthesizes natural-language unit-test criteria from the query, scores each candidate via LLM-as-a-judge, and selects the highest-scoring SQL.
Inter-stage dependencies of the CHESS workflow are explicitly represented so that a stage's LLM requests are only dispatched after its prerequisites finish.
The MAC-SQL agentic workflow is implemented for the generalization experiment using QWEN3-30B on Trace 4.
Workload traces used in the evaluation have been constructed.
Trace 1 is constructed by sampling queries purely from the BIRD financial database following the 14%/72%/14% (simple/moderate/challenging) mix.
Trace 2 is constructed by sampling queries purely from the BIRD formula1 database with the same simple/moderate/challenging mix.
Trace 3 is constructed by sampling queries from both the financial and formula1 databases with the same complexity mix.
Trace 4 is constructed by sampling queries from Spider 1.0 across the 9 specified domain databases for the MAC-SQL/QWEN3-30B experiment.
Trace 5 is constructed by augmenting Trace 3 with 25% independent single-shot LLM inference requests, each carrying its own SLO.
Trace 6 is constructed by augmenting Trace 3 with 50% independent single-shot LLM inference requests, each carrying its own SLO.
Query arrivals are generated using a Poisson process with configurable arrival rate (e.g., 0.5 QPS, 1.0 QPS, 20 QPS, 30 QPS) to emulate stochastic user submissions.
A non-stationary arrival pattern (5 QPS to 20 QPS to 5 QPS) is implemented for the fluctuating-workload experiment.
The HEXGEN-FLOW two-level scheduling system has been implemented.
A centralized global coordinator process is implemented to receive end-to-end Text-to-SQL queries and dispatch individual LLM inference requests to model instances.
Multi-stage dependency management has been implemented in the coordinator.
The coordinator tracks per-query stage completion and dispatches a request only when its prerequisites have finished.
For parallelizable stages (e.g., candidate generation), ready requests are dispatched concurrently across available instances.
As stages complete, the coordinator updates each query's remaining end-to-end deadline so that downstream per-request SLO budgets are tightened accordingly.
The workload-balanced dispatching policy has been implemented in the global coordinator.
Output length is estimated for each incoming request using a prediction function L_hat_out(q_i,j) derived from input length, following the method of Zheng et al.
Per-instance empirical performance profiles for prefill and decode phases (t_prefill^m and t_decode^m) are maintained and used to compute t_comp_{i,j}^m = t_prefill^m(L_in) + t_decode^m(L_hat_out).
The expected queuing cost t_queue_{i,j}^m is computed as the sum of estimated execution times of all requests currently queued at instance m.
The dispatching heuristic score combining computation cost and queuing cost (parameterized by alpha and the deployment-specific normalization constant beta) is implemented per Equation 4.
beta = C_ref * Q_ref is calibrated once per deployment using a few warm-up queries and then held fixed.
For each incoming request, the coordinator selects the model instance with the highest heuristic score and dispatches the request there.
On instance timeout/failure, the attempt is treated as failed and re-issued to another instance via the same workload-balanced dispatching policy.
The local urgency-driven priority queue has been implemented on every model instance.
The per-request SLO budget t^SLO_{i,j} is allocated proportionally to remaining downstream computation costs, normalized by (T_i^SLO - tau_elapsed^i), per Equation 5.
The average per-instance computation cost used in budget allocation is computed as the mean over all model instances of t_comp_{i,k}^m.
tau_elapsed^i is updated upon completion of each prior request q_{i,j-1} so that subsequent budgets are recomputed in real time (matching Figure 3's debit-and-reallocate behavior).
The urgency metric U_{i,j} = t_comp_{i,j}^m - (t^SLO_{i,j} - tau_{i,j}) is implemented and recomputed continuously as waiting time tau_{i,j} grows.
At batch boundaries during continuous batching, the local queue selects the pending request with the highest urgency (argmax over Theta^m) for inclusion in the next batch.
Unexpected execution cost overruns are absorbed into tau_elapsed^i, which tightens t^SLO_{i,j+1} for the subsequent request (debit-style reallocation in Figure 3).
The simulator-guided alpha-tuning loop has been implemented.
alpha is initialized to 0 at cold-start, prioritizing queue-length minimization, and execution traces (arrival times, queue delays, stage durations) are recorded.
Sliding-window monitoring computes 95th-percentile latency over a window sized to contain at least ~5 completed end-to-end queries and adapts to workload intensity.
A one-sided two-sample t-test compares the new window's tail latency against the prior baseline; tuning is triggered when the p-value falls below 0.01.
A trace-driven CPU-only simulator replays the most recent historical queries to estimate T_0.95(alpha) for each candidate alpha.
The simulator searches alpha in {0.0, 0.1, 0.2, ..., 1.0} and selects alpha* = argmin T_0.95(alpha).
Simulations are parallelized so that a typical tuning step completes in ~1.9-3.1 seconds at 0.5/1.0 QPS windows and ~3.9-6.6 seconds for 20/30 QPS windows.
All baseline serving systems used in the comparison have been implemented or integrated.
The vanilla vLLM baseline using FCFS local scheduling and round-robin global dispatching has been set up for end-to-end Text-to-SQL serving.
The VTC baseline is implemented, treating each end-to-end Text-to-SQL query as a 'user' and using token-count-based fairness scheduling.
The QLM baseline is implemented, grouping requests by SLO and using a waiting-time estimator to reorder request groups when SLOs are at risk.
The LLF baseline is implemented, scheduling each stage's requests by job laxity (deadline minus elapsed time and remaining processing time) without per-request budget recomputation.
The Ray Serve baseline is implemented, expressing the agentic Text-to-SQL DAG via chained deployments and using Power-of-Two-Choices routing.
Ablation variant RR+PQ (round-robin dispatching + HEXGEN-FLOW local priority queue) is implemented.
Ablation variant WB+FCFS (workload-balanced dispatching + FCFS local queue) is implemented.
Evaluation metrics and harness have been implemented.
Exclusive-environment end-to-end execution time is measured per query so that SLO scales can be applied multiplicatively.
SLO attainment is computed as the fraction of end-to-end queries completing within the scaled SLO and reported across SLO-scale sweeps.
P95 tail latency over end-to-end queries is computed and used as the headline scheduling metric.
System throughput is computed as the number of end-to-end Text-to-SQL queries completed per second.
For each run, the minimum SLO scale at which 95% attainment is reached is reported (matching the methodology of Figure 4 / Figure 6).
Jain's fairness index is computed over per-tenant SLO attainment rates in multi-tenant experiments.
Each end-to-end experiment is repeated with random seeds 0, 1, 2 and error bars are reported.
All end-to-end and ablation experiments described in Section 5 have been executed.
All six baselines plus HEXGEN-FLOW are evaluated on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 under Hetero-1 and Hetero-2 at 0.5 QPS and 1.0 QPS (matching the panels of Figure 4).
Throughput experiments (Figure 5) for all baselines plus HEXGEN-FLOW are executed on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 under both Hetero-1 and Hetero-2.
Homogeneous-cluster experiments (Figure 6) are executed for all baselines plus HEXGEN-FLOW on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 at 0.5 and 1.0 QPS.
Stress-test on Trace 3 at 30 QPS under Hetero-2 is executed for all baselines plus HEXGEN-FLOW.
Fluctuating-workload experiment (5 to 20 to 5 QPS, peak 20 QPS shown in Figure 7) is executed on Trace 3 under Hetero-2.
Alternative-workflow experiment with MAC-SQL + QWEN3-30B is executed on Trace 4 at 2.5 QPS and 5.0 QPS under Hetero-2.
Competing-workload experiments are executed on Trace 5 (25% competing) and Trace 6 (50% competing) at 1 QPS under Hetero-2.
Multi-tenant experiments with two users having distinct SLOs are executed on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 under Hetero-2 at 1.0 QPS.
Ablation runs WB+FCFS, RR+PQ, and WB+PQ are executed on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 under Hetero-1 and Hetero-2 at 0.5 and 1.0 QPS (matching Figure 9).
alpha-sweep experiments (Figure 10) sweep alpha in {0.0, 0.1, 0.2, 0.3, 0.4} on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3 under Hetero-1 and Hetero-2 at 0.5 QPS.
alpha-tuning overhead measurements (Table 4) are collected for all four (deployment, QPS) combinations on Trace 1, Trace 2, Trace 3.
The trace-characterization measurements reported in Section 5.1 have been reproduced.
Average number of LLM calls per end-to-end query on Trace 1 is approximately 19.4 (with variance ~12.1) over three trials.
Average number of LLM calls per end-to-end query on Trace 2 is approximately 20.7 (with variance ~14.7) over three trials.
Average number of LLM calls per end-to-end query on Trace 3 is approximately 21.1 (with variance ~16.3) over three trials.
Reported numerical claims about HEXGEN-FLOW vs. baselines are reproduced within tolerance.
Across all traces, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by a factor in the range 1.42x to 1.56x relative to state-of-the-art baselines (headline claim from abstract/conclusion).
Across all traces, HEXGEN-FLOW increases throughput by a factor in the range 1.49x to 1.81x relative to state-of-the-art baselines (headline claim).
Compared with vLLM, HEXGEN-FLOW achieves P95 tail-latency reductions in the range 19.2%-56.2% across the evaluated heterogeneous configurations.
Compared with vLLM, HEXGEN-FLOW achieves throughput improvements in the range 49.1%-81.0%.
Compared with VTC, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 19.2%-53.8% and increases throughput by 10.3%-39.8%.
Compared with QLM, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 14.0%-39.6% and increases throughput by 17.5%-36.6%.
Compared with LLF, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 12.4%-39.6% and increases throughput by 6.7%-59.8%.
Compared with Ray Serve, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 8.6%-23.8% and delivers 30.4%-38.2% higher throughput.
On Trace 1 with Hetero-1 at 0.5 QPS, vLLM requires ~5.4 SLO scales for 95% attainment while HEXGEN-FLOW requires only ~3.5 SLO scales.
On Trace 2 with Hetero-2 at 0.5 QPS, VTC requires ~7.9 SLO scales while HEXGEN-FLOW requires only ~5.2 SLO scales for 95% attainment.
On Trace 3 with Hetero-2 at 1.0 QPS, QLM requires ~7.8 SLO scales while HEXGEN-FLOW requires ~6.1 SLO scales for 95% attainment.
On Trace 3 with Hetero-2 at 1.0 QPS, LLF requires ~7.6 SLO scales while HEXGEN-FLOW reaches 95% attainment at ~5.2 SLO scales.
On Trace 3 with Hetero-2 at 1.0 QPS, Ray Serve requires ~5.9 SLO scales while HEXGEN-FLOW reaches 95% attainment at ~5.2 SLO scales.
Homogeneous-environment numerical claims are reproduced.
In Homo deployments HEXGEN-FLOW achieves up to 32.6%, 31.4%, 28.6%, 47.3%, and 19.2% lower P95 tail latency than vLLM, VTC, QLM, LLF, and Ray respectively.
On Trace 3 in Homo at 1.0 QPS, baselines require at least ~7.2 SLO scales for 95% attainment while HEXGEN-FLOW requires only ~5.9.
Numerical claims from auxiliary experiments (Figure 7) are reproduced.
Under the heavy 30 QPS and the fluctuating (peak 20 QPS) workloads, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 5.2%-23.3% versus all baselines.
During the fluctuating workload, alpha-tuning is triggered exactly twice, coinciding with the two workload shifts.
On MAC-SQL with QWEN3-30B (Trace 4), HEXGEN-FLOW achieves P95 tail-latency reductions in the range 34.0%-38.3% versus baselines.
Under 25% (Trace 5) and 50% (Trace 6) competing-workload mixes, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by approximately 18.2%-36.4% versus baselines.
Multi-tenant fairness and ablation numerical claims are reproduced.
In the two-user multi-tenant evaluation, HEXGEN-FLOW reduces P95 tail latency by 27.9%-35.7% for users with different SLO requirements versus baselines.
Jain's fairness index over per-tenant SLO attainment across Trace 1/2/3 is approximately 0.98.
WB+PQ achieves 10.5%-34.8% lower P95 tail latency than RR+PQ across the ablation sweep, confirming the contribution of workload-balanced dispatching.
WB+PQ achieves up to 48% lower P95 tail latency than WB+FCFS, confirming the contribution of the urgency-driven local priority queue.
On Trace 3 with Hetero-1 at 0.5 QPS, RR+PQ requires ~5.8 SLO scales for 95% attainment while WB+PQ requires only ~4.3 (~35% reduction).
On Trace 2 with Hetero-2 at 0.5 QPS, WB+FCFS requires ~5.8 SLO scales for 95% attainment while WB+PQ requires only ~4.6.
alpha-tuning numerical claims are reproduced.
The optimal alpha on Trace 1 is approximately 0.1 under Hetero-1 and 0.3 under Hetero-2.
The optimal alpha on Trace 2 is approximately 0.2 under Hetero-1 and 0.3 under Hetero-2.
The optimal alpha on Trace 3 is approximately 0.2 under Hetero-1 and 0.4 under Hetero-2, with the latter yielding ~14% improvement at 95% SLO attainment over alpha=0.
alpha-tuning overhead at 0.5/1.0 QPS lies in approximately 1.9-3.1 seconds per 100-second window, and tuning is triggered in roughly 28.6%-40.0% of windows.
Under stress tests at 20/30 QPS, alpha-tuning takes 3.9-6.6 seconds per 300-second window with trigger rate 28.6%-33.3%.
Case-study analyses on dispatching and local-queue behavior are reproduced.
Per-stage task distributions across A100/A6000/L40 instances under WB versus RR on Trace 3/Hetero-2/0.5 QPS are logged and approximately match the percentages reported in Table 2.

A snapshot showing local-queue urgency values (matching the qualitative ordering of Table 3, where request 6 with the highest urgency is selected ahead of earlier-arrived requests) is logged for the PQ case study.
